flashcards.back: Thread scheduling is the OS mechanism that determines which thread gets CPU time when multiple threads compete for execution. It decides the order and duration for which threads run on available CPU cores.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between thread scheduling and process scheduling?
flashcards.back: Process scheduling allocates CPU time between different processes, while thread scheduling allocates CPU time between threads within a process or across processes. Thread scheduling has lower overhead since threads share same address space.

flashcards.front: List the five scheduling criteria used to evaluate algorithms.
flashcards.back: 1) CPU Utilization - percentage of time CPU is busy 2) Throughput - processes completed per unit time 3) Turnaround Time - time from submission to completion 4) Waiting Time - time spent ready in queue 5) Response Time - time from request to first response

FCFS schedules threads in the order they arrive in the ready queue. The first thread to request CPU gets CPU time first. It is simple but can cause convoy effect where short threads wait behind long ones.

flashcards.front: What is SJF (Shortest Job First) scheduling?
flashcards.back: SJF selects the thread with smallest burst time from ready queue. It minimizes average waiting time but requires knowing burst times in advance. Can cause starvation of long threads.

Round Robin assigns time quantum (slice) to each thread in circular order. When quantum expires, thread goes to back of queue. Provides fair allocation and good response time for time-sharing systems.

User-level threads are threads managed entirely by user-level thread library without OS kernel involvement. They are fast to create and switch but cannot utilize multiprocessors; if one thread blocks, all threads in process block.

Kernel-level threads are managed directly by OS kernel. OS scheduler handles them and can schedule multiple threads on multiple processors. Creation and context switching is slower than user-level threads.

flashcards.front: What is the many-to-one threading model?
flashcards.back: Many user-level threads map to single kernel thread. Thread management done in user space; if one user thread makes blocking system call, entire process blocks. Used in historical systems like Green Threads.

flashcards.front: What is the one-to-one threading model?
flashcards.back: Each user-level thread maps to one kernel-level thread. Provides true parallelism, any thread can block without affecting others. Used in Linux, Windows. Drawback is creating many kernel threads can be resource-intensive.

flashcards.front: What is the many-to-many threading model?
flashcards.back: Multiple user-level threads multiplex to equal or fewer kernel threads. Combines benefits of both: parallelism without resource limits, and any thread can make blocking calls without blocking entire process.

flashcards.front: What is thread context switching overhead?
flashcards.back: When OS switches between threads, it must save/restore CPU registers, program counter, stack pointer, and thread-local storage. This overhead is lower than process context switch since threads share address space and resources.
Thread affinity binds a thread to specific CPU core(s). Improves cache performance by keeping thread on same core where its data is cached. Can be soft (hint) or hard (mandatory) in modern OS.

Real-time scheduling ensures threads meet deadlines. Hard real-time requires guaranteed completion by deadline; soft real-time attempts to meet deadlines but missing is tolerable. Uses priority-based preemptive scheduling.

Multiple-processor scheduling assigns threads to multiple CPU cores. Considerations include load balancing, affinity, and whether cores share cache. Approaches include symmetric (all cores equal) and asymmetric (specific cores for specific tasks).
